text: The patient is positioned in lithotomy after induction of general anesthesia. A three-centimeter transumbilical incision is made, and a surgical glove port is used as a single-access device. Disposable trocars are tied into the glove fingers, and the glove cuff is stretched onto a wound protector retractor placed in the incision. Carbon dioxide insufflation is initiated through one of the trocars to establish pneumoperitoneum.

The laparoscopic view through the glove port reveals an anastomotic stricture with creeping fat visible on the distal ileum. The surgical field shows a relative absence of postoperative peritoneal adhesions.

The surgeon retracts the omentum capillate above the transverse colon with the patient positioned head down and right side up. The ileocolonic segment is mobilized from lateral to medial, revealing a densely adhered ileocolonic anastomosis to the lateral peritoneum. The surgeon uses a ligature for tissue dissection and avoids direct bowel grasping, opting for closed instrument retraction or gentle traction on the mesentery or mesocolon. The intention is to perform an extracorporeal anastomosis via the single port access site.

The surgeon utilizes a single-port laparoscopic approach with a surgical glove port, focusing on one instrument at a time. A 30-degree lens is used to minimize instrument clash, and the EndoEye system provides high-definition optics. During lateral dissection, the gonadal vessels and right ureter are visualized, ensuring the preservation of the fascia overlying these structures. The vermiculation of the erasure is clearly visible in the slim patient.

The surgeon retracts the omentum cephalad and elevates the peritoneal attachments towards the anterior abdominal wall, allowing the transverse colon to descend due to gravity. The proximal transverse colon is mobilized to facilitate distal mobilization for extracorporeal staple anastomosis. The duodenum is visible and protected during this process, with the camera providing an off-centered view typical of single-port laparoscopic operations.

The surgeon mobilizes the omentum off the transverse colon, extending the dissection beyond the falciform ligament. Attention is then directed to the distal ileal mesentery, ensuring sufficient mobilization of IST-II. The ureter's position is carefully checked due to potential distortion from previous surgery, minimizing the risk of injury. The final piece of post-operative fibrous tissue is divided, followed by dissection of a virgin tissue plane to achieve optimal mobilization of the diseased segment.

The surgeon returns the small and large bowel to their previous anatomical positions, ensuring correct orientation for extraction. The drug port is removed, and laparoscopic graspers are replaced with Babcock tissue holding forceps. The outer ring of the wound protector is adjusted to maximize the extraction aperture, allowing the bowel to be withdrawn easily. The strictured anastomosis is visible. After resection and construction of a stapled side-to-side ileocolonic anastomosis, relaparoscopy confirms hemostasis and the correct positioning of the anastomosis, with the operation appearing bloodless.
